# Molecular Biology and Risk Stratification

## Cell of Origin Classification

Diffuse large B-cell lymphoma (DLBCL) is a biologically heterogeneous disease, and understanding its molecular underpinnings is essential for risk stratification and treatment selection. The foundational molecular classification divides DLBCL into two principal subtypes based on the putative cell of origin (COO): germinal center B-cell-like (GCB) and activated B-cell-like (ABC) [@Li2018Diffuse]. Gene expression profiling (GEP) was the original method used to define these subtypes, revealing that GCB-DLBCL arises from centroblasts within the germinal center, while ABC-DLBCL derives from post-germinal center B cells poised for plasmablastic differentiation. Patients with the GCB subtype generally experience superior outcomes compared with those harboring ABC-DLBCL when treated with standard R-CHOP chemoimmunotherapy, with approximately 10--15% of cases remaining unclassifiable [@Li2018Diffuse; @SusanibarAdaniya2021].

Because GEP requires fresh or frozen tissue and is not universally available, immunohistochemistry (IHC)-based algorithms have been developed as surrogates. The Hans algorithm, which employs CD10, BCL6, and MUM1/IRF4 staining, is the most widely used classifier in routine clinical practice. While the Hans algorithm achieves approximately 80% concordance with GEP-based COO assignment, it has recognized limitations in accuracy, particularly for borderline cases [@Staiger2017Clinical]. The COO distinction has significant therapeutic implications, as several targeted agents demonstrate preferential activity in ABC-DLBCL, including BTK inhibitors and immunomodulatory drugs [@SusanibarAdaniya2021].

## Molecular Subtypes and the LymphGen Classifier

Beyond the binary COO classification, recent whole-exome sequencing studies have revealed that DLBCL comprises at least five to seven distinct genetic subtypes characterized by co-occurring genetic alterations. Lacy et al. performed targeted sequencing on a large population-based cohort (n = 928) and identified five molecular subtypes termed MYD88, BCL2, SOCS1/SGK1, TET2/SGK1, and NOTCH2, along with an unclassified group [@Lacy2020Targeted]. These subtypes demonstrated distinct prognostic profiles: the MYD88 subtype conferred poor prognosis, the NOTCH2 subtype intermediate prognosis, and the BCL2 subtype favorable prognosis.

The LymphGen classifier, developed from complementary genomic studies, defines analogous subtypes designated MCD (MYD88/CD79B co-mutations), BN2 (BCL6 fusions and NOTCH2 mutations), N1 (NOTCH1 mutations), EZB (EZH2 mutations and BCL2 translocations), and ST2 (SGK1 and TET2 mutations) [@Wilson2021Effect]. The MCD and N1 subtypes fall predominantly within the ABC-DLBCL category and are characterized by chronic active B-cell receptor signaling, rendering them potentially susceptible to BTK inhibition. Wilson et al. demonstrated that the 3-year event-free survival of younger patients (age 60 years or younger) with MCD and N1 subtypes treated with ibrutinib plus R-CHOP reached 100%, compared with 42.9% and 50%, respectively, for R-CHOP alone [@Wilson2021Effect]. The EZB subtype, predominantly GCB in origin, harbors recurrent EZH2 gain-of-function mutations and may respond to EZH2 inhibitors such as tazemetostat. This molecular taxonomy is moving toward clinical application, with the potential to guide subtype-specific therapeutic strategies.

## Double-Hit and Triple-Hit Lymphoma

High-grade B-cell lymphoma with concurrent rearrangements of MYC and BCL2 and/or BCL6---designated double-hit lymphoma (DHL) or triple-hit lymphoma (THL)---represents a particularly aggressive subset. Ennishi et al. developed a 104-gene double-hit signature (DHITsig) that assigned 27% of GCB-DLBCLs to a DHITsig-positive group, of which only half harbored confirmed MYC and BCL2 rearrangements by fluorescence in situ hybridization (FISH) [@Ennishi2019Doublehit]. DHITsig-positive patients had significantly inferior outcomes after R-CHOP, with a 5-year time-to-progression rate of 57% compared with 81% for DHITsig-negative patients (P < .001) [@Ennishi2019Doublehit]. Sha et al. further defined a molecular high-grade (MHG) group comprising 9% of DLBCL cases, with MYC-rearranged and double-hit cases strongly over-represented but accounting for only half of this poor-risk population [@Sha2019Molecular]. Sole MYC rearrangement, without concurrent BCL2 or BCL6 involvement, has also been associated with adverse prognosis, though its independent prognostic significance remains debated [@Landsburg2016Sole]. Dual expression of MYC and BCL2 protein by IHC (so-called double-expressor lymphoma) identifies a broader poor-risk group that partially overlaps with but is distinct from genetically defined DHL [@Staiger2017Clinical].

## Prognostic Indices

Clinical risk stratification in DLBCL relies primarily on the International Prognostic Index (IPI), which incorporates age, serum lactate dehydrogenase (LDH), Ann Arbor stage, number of extranodal sites, and Eastern Cooperative Oncology Group (ECOG) performance status. Ruppert et al. evaluated 2,124 DLBCL patients treated with frontline R-CHOP across seven randomized trials and compared the IPI, revised IPI (R-IPI), and NCCN-IPI [@Ruppert2020International]. Five-year overall survival estimates ranged from 49% to 92% using the NCCN-IPI, which demonstrated the greatest discriminatory power (concordance index = 0.632) compared with the standard IPI (0.626) and R-IPI (0.590) [@Ruppert2020International].

The CNS-IPI, developed by Schmitz et al. from analysis of 2,164 patients with aggressive B-cell lymphoma, adds kidney and/or adrenal gland involvement to the standard IPI factors to predict central nervous system (CNS) relapse [@Schmitz2016CNS]. The model defines three risk groups with 2-year CNS disease rates of 0.6% (low risk, 46% of patients), 3.4% (intermediate risk, 41%), and 10.2% (high risk, 12%) [@Schmitz2016CNS]. This tool is now widely used to identify patients who may benefit from CNS-directed prophylaxis.

## Key Genetic Mutations

Recurrent somatic mutations provide further biologic and prognostic insight. TP53 mutations, found in approximately 20% of DLBCL cases, are associated with adverse outcomes across COO subtypes. MYD88 L265P and CD79B mutations are hallmarks of the MCD subtype and activate NF-kB signaling through the toll-like receptor and B-cell receptor pathways, respectively [@Pal2018Role]. EZH2 gain-of-function mutations characterize the EZB subtype and promote aberrant epigenetic silencing of tumor suppressor genes. CREBBP mutations, which impair histone acetyltransferase activity and disrupt the germinal center reaction, occur in approximately 15--20% of GCB-DLBCL cases. Liquid biopsy approaches using cell-free DNA (cfDNA) have emerged as a promising tool for non-invasive genotyping, with ultra-deep targeted sequencing detecting DLBCL-associated mutations present in greater than 20% of tumor alleles with over 90% sensitivity and near-perfect specificity [@Rossi2017Diffuse]. Longitudinal monitoring of cfDNA during R-CHOP treatment has demonstrated rapid mutation clearance in responding patients, suggesting potential utility for minimal residual disease assessment [@Rossi2017Diffuse].
